insights.insight: Admissions peak in the [70-80) bracket (26,068 encounters). Patients aged 60+ account for 67.4% of all admissions, reflecting the elderly-heavy diabetic inpatient population typical of US hospitals.
Females represent 53.8% of admissions. 30-day readmission rates: Female 11.25% vs Male 11.06%. Gender differences are modest; age and prior utilization are stronger predictors.

Overall 30-day readmission rate is 11.16% — a key CMS penalty metric. An additional 34.9% are readmitted after 30 days. Highest-risk age bracket: [20-30). Hospitals should target discharge planning for elderly high-utilization patients.

Circulatory_390_459 is the most common primary diagnosis (30,336 cases). Supplementary_V has the highest readmission rate (16.2%) among high-volume groups. Care pathways for circulatory and diabetes complications should be prioritized for readmission reduction programs.

Average length of stay is 4.4 days (median 4). LOS-readmission correlation is 0.044. Longer stays often indicate clinical complexity; extended stays beyond 7 days warrant enhanced discharge planning and home health coordination.

insulin is the most frequently active medication (54,383 cases). Patients on insulin have a 12.1% readmission rate vs 10.0% without insulin, indicating insulin use as a complexity marker. Polypharmacy (16+ meds) correlates with elevated readmission risk.

Strongest correlation with 30-day readmission: number_inpatient (r=0.165). Prior utilization (inpatient/emergency visits) and medication count cluster together, confirming that high-utilization diabetic patients are the primary readmission risk cohort. These features should be weighted heavily in the ML model.
